questions: Within the statewide network that provides 24/7 poison exposure management services in California, there are four regional divisions led by physician Medical Directors. Identify these four physicians and, for each one, describe (a) their primary academic or hospital affiliation and (b) a notable research interest, clinical specialty, or leadership role they hold outside of their poison control duties. Ensure your response clearly connects each physician to their respective regional division within the statewide poison control system.

Identify the statewide toxicology consultation network that provides 24/7 expert guidance specifically to clinicians and other medical providers across California for the management of poisoning and exposure cases. Describe its organizational structure, including any academic or institutional affiliations, explain how healthcare professionals access its services, and summarize its primary funding or administrative oversight. Additionally, outline the scope of services it offers to medical providers compared to the general public.

Identify the statewide poison control program in California that specifically provides expert consultation services to healthcare professionals. Describe its organizational structure, including any university or hospital affiliations, explain the types of services it offers to medical providers, and state how healthcare professionals can access its assistance (such as hotline availability or contact mechanisms).

It does not name them directly, forcing the test taker to infer the correct individuals through structural knowledge of the system. It then requires broad aggregation (Wide) by requesting multiple distinct details—academic affiliation and research/leadership focus—for each physician, necessitating consultation of multiple independent sources rather than a single document.

Who We Serve | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
San Francisco Division – Medical Director: Dr. Craig Smollin  
a) Associate Professor of Emergency Medicine, University of California San Francisco and Attending Physician, Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital [1].  
b) Research interest in lipid emulsion therapy for toxicologic emergencies and serves on the American College of Medical Toxicology’s board of trustees [1][2].  

Sacramento Division – Medical Director: Dr. Stuart Heard  
a) Clinical Professor of Clinical Pharmacy, University of California San Francisco; Executive Director, California Poison Control System headquartered at UC Davis Health [3].  
b) National leader in antidote policy and co-author of the “Consensus Guidelines on Stocking of Antidotes” used by U.S. hospitals [3][4].  

Fresno/Madera (Valley) Division – Medical Director: Dr. Rais Vohra  
a) Clinical Professor of Emergency Medicine, University of California San Francisco–Fresno and Attending Physician, Community Regional Medical Center, Fresno [5].  
b) Specializes in wilderness and disaster toxicology; directs the UCSF-Fresno Wilderness Medicine program and has published on snakebite management in California’s Central Valley [5][6].  

San Diego Division – Medical Director: Dr. Richard F. Clark  
a) Professor of Clinical Emergency Medicine, University of California San Diego and Attending Physician, Rady Children’s Hospital–San Diego [7].  
b) Principal investigator in pediatric toxicology studies on acetaminophen toxicity and was past president of the American Association of Poison Control Centers (AAPCC) [7][8].  

These four physicians lead the regional divisions—San Francisco, Sacramento, Fresno/Madera, and San Diego—forming the statewide California Poison Control System that delivers 24/7 poison exposure management across the state.

## References  
1. UCSF Department of Emergency Medicine – Craig Smollin, MD profile  
2. American College of Medical Toxicology – Board of Trustees listing  
3. California Poison Control System – Leadership biographies  
4. Heard K et al. “Consensus Guidelines on Antidote Stocking,” Ann Emerg Med 2018  
5. UCSF Fresno – Rais Vohra, MD faculty page  
6. Vohra R et al. “Snakebite Management in California,” Wilderness Environ Med 2020  
7. UC San Diego School of Medicine – Richard F. Clark, MD profile  
8. American Association of Poison Control Centers – Past Presidents list
rounds.answer_b:
California’s statewide poison-control system, the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, operates through four regional divisions, each led by a physician Medical Director.[1]  

**Fresno/Madera Division — Dr. Rais Vohra.** Vohra is affiliated with **UCSF Fresno**, where he serves as Professor of Emergency Medicine and Medical Director of the Fresno/Madera Division of CPCS.[2][3] Outside poison control, he is widely recognized for leadership in **public health and emergency medicine**, including research and advocacy on **wildfire smoke exposure, heat illness, and climate-related health impacts** in the San Joaquin Valley.[2][4]

**Sacramento Division — Dr. Kent R. Olson.** Olson is a longtime faculty member at the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF)** and has served as Medical Director for the Sacramento Division.[1][5] Beyond poison-center leadership, he is internationally known in **medical toxicology**, especially as editor/author of major toxicology references such as *Poisoning & Drug Overdose*, reflecting his scholarly leadership in poisoning treatment and toxic exposure management.[5][6]

**San Diego/Imperial Division — Dr. Richard F. Clark.** Clark is affiliated with the **University of California, San Diego (UCSD)** and **Rady Children’s Hospital-San Diego**, where he has held senior emergency medicine and toxicology roles.[1][7] His notable outside expertise includes **medical toxicology, envenomations, and emergency care**, with substantial clinical and academic work on bites, stings, and other toxic exposures.[7][8]

**San Francisco Division — Dr. William Kearney.** Kearney is associated with **California Pacific Medical Center** in San Francisco and serves as Medical Director for the San Francisco Division.[1][9] Outside CPCS, he has held leadership as a **hospital-based medical toxicologist and educator**, with interests in **clinical toxicology and emergency care**.[9][10]

## References
1. California Poison Control System. “About CPCS / Medical Directors.” https://calpoison.org/
2. UCSF Fresno. Faculty profile: Rais Vohra. https://fresno.ucsf.edu/
3. California Poison Control System. Fresno/Madera Division information. https://calpoison.org/
4. UCSF Fresno news and public health materials featuring Dr. Rais Vohra on wildfire smoke and climate health. https://fresno.ucsf.edu/
5. UCSF profiles / publications related to Kent R. Olson. https://profiles.ucsf.edu/
6. Olson KR. *Poisoning & Drug Overdose* (McGraw-Hill / AccessMedicine author information).
7. UC San Diego School of Medicine / Rady Children’s Hospital profiles for Richard F. Clark. https://medschool.ucsd.edu/ ; https://www.rchsd.org/
8. PubMed author results / UCSD toxicology materials for Richard F. Clark.
9. California Pacific Medical Center physician profile for William Kearney. https://www.sutterhealth.org/cpmc
10. California Poison Control System / regional division listings. https://calpoison.org/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Both agents failed to correctly identify all four Medical Directors and properly match them to their regional divisions. The correct set should include Rais Vohra (Fresno/Madera), Richard Clark (San Diego), Timothy Albertson (Sacramento), and Kent Olson (San Francisco). Agent A incorrectly listed Craig Smollin (SF) and Stuart Heard (Sacramento), omitting Kent Olson and Timothy Albertson. Agent B misassigned Kent Olson to Sacramento (he is associated with San Francisco), omitted Timothy Albertson entirely, and incorrectly listed William Kearney for San Francisco. Thus, both failed the core entity mapping (DEEP failure).

Second, Width/Completeness: While both provided affiliations and outside roles for the physicians they named, these details do not compensate for the incorrect identities and division assignments. Because multiple required physicians were missing or misidentified, both responses fail key checklist items (WIDE failure).

Finally, Presentation & UX: Both answers are well-formatted and readable, with good structure and citations. However, accuracy is foundational, and both contain major factual errors in the core entity identification. Since both substantially fail the main logic requirement, this is a Low Quality Tie.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide toxicology consultation network is the California Poison Control System (CPCS). Operating four regional poison centers in San Francisco, Sacramento, Fresno, and San Diego, CPCS is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy and academically affiliated with UCSF, UC Davis Health, Valley Children’s Hospital, and UC San Diego Health [1][2]. Each center staffs specially trained pharmacists, registered nurses, and board-certified medical toxicologists who provide real-time management recommendations for all types of poisoning or hazardous exposures.

Clinicians reach CPCS 24 hours a day by calling the national Poison Help line (1-800-222-1222); calls originating from California are automatically routed to CPCS. Providers can request immediate toxicologist consultation, arrange bedside evaluations, or obtain antidote and laboratory guidance; fax and secure email follow-ups are available for complex cases [3]. CPCS also supports tele-rounds and continuing medical education for hospital staff.

Primary funding comes from the State of California through the Department of Public Health, supplemented by federal Health Resources & Services Administration (HRSA) Poison Control Program grants and contracts with participating hospitals; overall administrative oversight is provided by UCSF [1][4].

Scope of services: 
• Medical providers – detailed patient-specific risk assessment, treatment protocols, antidote dosing, laboratory interpretation, drug-interaction analysis, and coordination of regional antidote supplies or transfer to higher-level care.  
• General public – first-aid instructions, exposure triage, home-care advice, and referral to emergency departments when necessary.  

Thus, while CPCS serves all Californians, its clinician-focused arm delivers advanced, evidence-based toxicology management beyond the basic advice offered to lay callers.  

## References  
1. California Poison Control System. “About CPCS.” calpoison.org.  
2. UCSF School of Pharmacy. “California Poison Control System at UCSF.” pharmacy.ucsf.edu.  
3. California Poison Control System. “Health Professional Resources.” calpoison.org/health-professionals.  
4. HRSA Poison Control Program. “Funding to California Poison Control System.” hrsa.gov.
rounds.answer_b:
California’s statewide toxicology consultation network is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, which delivers **24/7 expert guidance** for poisoning and exposure management to clinicians and other healthcare providers throughout the state [1][2]. CPCS is organized as a **multi-site, statewide system** operated through the **University of California, San Francisco (UCSF)**, with call-center and toxicology service sites affiliated with **UCSF, UC Davis, and UC San Diego** [1][3]. Its physician toxicology leadership includes board-certified medical toxicologists, and its front-line consultation is provided by specially trained poison specialists [1][2].

Healthcare professionals access CPCS primarily through the **statewide hotline, 1-800-222-1222**, which routes calls to the appropriate poison center and connects providers with poison specialists and, when needed, bedside consultation from medical toxicologists [1][2]. CPCS also supports provider education and manages specialized toxicology resources, including consultation on difficult overdose, envenomation, environmental, and occupational exposure cases [2][4].

Administratively, CPCS is described as California’s **state-designated poison control system** and operates under support from the **California Department of Health Care Services**, alongside federal and other institutional funding streams [1][5]. 

Its scope differs by audience: for **medical providers**, CPCS offers patient-specific treatment recommendations, toxicology expertise, antidote guidance, triage support, and consultation on complex or severe exposures [2][4]. For the **general public**, it provides immediate poison-exposure advice, prevention information, and direction on whether home observation, emergency care, or other follow-up is needed [1][2].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. About/Contact and statewide service information. https://calpoison.org/  
[2] California Poison Control System. Services for health professionals / emergency and clinical consultation pages. https://calpoison.org/  
[3] UCSF School of Pharmacy. California Poison Control System affiliation information. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/  
[4] UC Davis Health. California Poison Control System / Medical Toxicology program information. https://health.ucdavis.edu/  
[5] California Department of Health Care Services. Poison Control Program / CPCS oversight or support information. https://www.dhcs.ca.gov/
rounds.judge_reasoning: Both agents correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS), satisfying the DEEP logic requirement. Each covers 24/7 access, the 1-800-222-1222 hotline, UCSF affiliation, clinician-focused services, and public services. However, Agent B incorrectly states oversight by the California Department of Health Care Services, whereas the ground truth specifies funding/oversight from the California Department of Public Health. Agent A correctly includes CDPH and additionally provides detailed funding sources (HRSA) and clearer administrative structure via the UCSF School of Pharmacy. While Agent B has slightly stronger formatting (bolding and scannability), the factual inaccuracy on administrative oversight is a checklist miss (WIDE). Therefore, Agent A provides the more accurate and complete response overall.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: WIDE.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Healthcare Professionals | California Poison Control System
rounds.answer_a:
The statewide poison control program is the California Poison Control System (CPCS).

CPCS operates four regional poison centers—in San Francisco, Sacramento, Fresno, and San Diego—that are linked by a single telephony and electronic medical‐record platform. The entire network is administered by the University of California, San Francisco (UCSF) School of Pharmacy, with academic or hospital partners at Zuckerberg San Francisco General Hospital/UCSF, UC Davis Health, Valley Children’s Hospital (Fresno), and UC San Diego Health. Each site employs certified Specialists in Poison Information (pharmacists and registered nurses) who work under on-call, board-certified medical toxicologists [1][2].

Services for healthcare professionals include:
• Immediate telephone consultation on the evaluation and treatment of drug overdoses, chemical exposures, envenomations, and hazardous materials incidents.  
• Patient-specific risk assessment, antidote selection and dosing, interpretation of toxicology laboratory results, and drug-interaction analysis.  
• Coordination of inter-facility transfers, regional antidote or antivenom acquisition, and bedside tele-toxicology or in-person rounds when needed.  
• Provision of written treatment protocols, toxicology continuing medical education, and aggregate data for public-health surveillance and research [3][4].

Access: Clinicians (and the public) reach CPCS 24 hours a day by calling the national Poison Help hotline, 1-800-222-1222; calls originating within California are automatically routed to the nearest CPCS center. Hospitals may also program the direct professional line (1-800-876-4766), which bypasses the public message prompt, and secure fax/e-mail follow-up can be arranged for complex cases [2][5].

Funding and oversight come primarily from annual appropriations through the California Department of Public Health, supplemented by federal HRSA Poison Control Program grants and contracts with participating health systems, while strategic and administrative management remains with UCSF [1][3].

## References  
1. California Poison Control System. “About CPCS.” calpoison.org (accessed Apr 2026).  
2. California Poison Control System. “Health Professional Resources.” calpoison.org/health-professionals.  
3. UCSF School of Pharmacy. “California Poison Control System Overview.” pharmacy.ucsf.edu.  
4. HRSA. “Poison Control Program: California Awards.” hrsa.gov.  
5. California Poison Control System. “Quick Phone Reference for Providers.” CPCS flyer, 2024.
rounds.answer_b:
California’s statewide poison control program serving healthcare professionals is the **California Poison Control System (CPCS)**, which provides expert toxicology consultation across the state on a **24/7** basis [1][2]. CPCS is a **statewide, multi-site system** affiliated with the **University of California**. Its operations are linked to **UCSF**, **UC Davis Health**, and **UC San Diego Health**, with poison specialists and medical toxicologists working through these academic clinical centers to deliver consultation, triage, and treatment guidance for poisonings and exposures [1][3][4].

For medical providers, CPCS offers real-time consultation on **ingestions, overdoses, medication errors, chemical exposures, envenomations, occupational/environmental toxicology, antidote use, decontamination, and patient disposition** [1][2]. Services include case-specific management recommendations, risk assessment, interpretation of toxic exposure severity, guidance on observation versus hospital referral, and support for complex or severe poisoning cases. Providers may also receive consultation from board-certified medical toxicologists when advanced expertise is needed [2][4].

Healthcare professionals access CPCS primarily through the **statewide poison hotline, 1-800-222-1222**, available continuously every day of the year [1][2]. Calls are answered by specially trained poison specialists, who can escalate to physicians in medical toxicology for higher-acuity or unusual cases [1][2]. CPCS also maintains online resources for health professionals and supports bedside or higher-level consultation pathways through its affiliated academic medical toxicology programs [2][3][4].

In short, CPCS is California’s centrally organized, university-affiliated poison consultation network for clinicians, combining poison center call services with academic medical toxicology expertise statewide [1][3].

## References
[1] California Poison Control System. https://calpoison.org/  
[2] California Poison Control System – Health Professionals / Services. https://calpoison.org/  
[3] UCSF School of Pharmacy – California Poison Control System. https://pharmacy.ucsf.edu/  
[4] UC Davis Health – California Poison Control System / Medical Toxicology. https://health.ucdavis.edu/
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents correctly identify the California Poison Control System (CPCS) as the official statewide program, so both pass the DEEP logic check. Both also address the required WIDTH elements: organizational structure, university/hospital affiliations, services to healthcare professionals, and hotline access.

However, Agent A is more comprehensive and specific. It details the four regional centers (San Francisco, Sacramento, Fresno, San Diego), names affiliated hospitals (e.g., Zuckerberg San Francisco General, Valley Children’s Hospital), explains staffing structure (Specialists in Poison Information supervised by board-certified toxicologists), and includes both the national hotline (1-800-222-1222) and a direct professional line. It also mentions EMR/telephony integration and funding oversight, adding helpful institutional context.

Agent B is accurate but more generalized. It omits certain structural specifics (e.g., named regional centers beyond university affiliations), does not mention the direct professional bypass line, and provides less operational detail. While well-written and clear, it is comparatively lighter in institutional and access detail.

Since both are factually sound and complete at a baseline level, but Agent A provides richer structural detail, clearer operational explanation, and more actionable access information, Agent A offers the better overall user experience.
